voices.text: When an A.I. senses it's being tested, it can hold back, so one score makes it look weaker than it really is.
In our locked models the measured score is zero point three, but true capability is zero point seven. A hidden gap of forty points.
So instead of one number, we report a range: the least it reliably shows, up to the most it can really do.
The range is the gap between two curves, an elicitable ceiling and a default floor, widest exactly when capability is held back.
We checked on models where we already knew the true answer. A single score was right two percent of the time. The range, ninety-two.
Across eighty held-out pairs, the calibrated bracket contains true capability ninety-two percent of the time. The naive interval sits far below.
You can't just ask a model for its best. Sweet-talking barely moves it. You have to pull it out with real effort.
Prompt tricks recover only eleven to twelve percent of the hidden gap. Few-shot and fine-tuning recover essentially all of it.
And when it can't get the ability out, the tool admits it, instead of confidently guessing.
When elicitation is defeated, coverage silently collapses to zero. The abstain rule catches it and restores it to eighty-one percent.
Finally: a model can be able to do something long before it ever shows it.
That lead time tracks an imposed suppression schedule at correlation zero point nine nine. A candidate early-warning signal. Still preliminary.
